14 min read
0:00 / 14:21
Let’s start by orienting to where we are in the TDB process.
By now, you should have:
▪ A reasonably accurate description of a minimum viable
audience you seek to serve (initially).
▪ An in-depth understanding of that audience’s needs and
desires (expressed in their own words).
▪ At least one expression of a value-creating idea to share with
that audience for initial feedback.
▪ Minimum viable infrastructure to support hypothesis-
testing.
▪ A marketing hypothesis to test.
If you don’t have all of these ingredients yet, that’s OK. However,
this module requires everything in the list above before you can
move forward effectively.
We’re going to use Facebook’s advertising platform to generate
highly-targeted awareness for our ideas. That awareness provides
the fuel to test our experiments and the data to validate and
improve upon our ideas before we’ve committed signi�cant time,
Validate, Part IV (FB Testing
Traf�c Framework)
MP3 File 4.93 MB
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 13 --

energy, and dollars.
There are other ways to generate traf�c, but we believe that
Facebook is the best option for validating ideas for several
reasons.
First, we can target audiences narrowly using demographics (for
example, age, gender, geography) and psychographics (for
example, interests). The more clearly we know who we’re
advertising to the more meaningful the results will be.
Second, Facebook’s advertising platform allows long-copy ads (700
— 900 words will be our approximate target). We can ‘make our
case’ in our ads effectively which attracts the right prospects and
repels the wrong prospects.
Third, Facebook provides data that creates more texture in our
results. For example, we’ll know who clicks on our ads to read
more vs. who clicks on our ads to go to a landing page.
We’ll also see when our ads are shared (which is a strong social
signal indicating value), and various audience reactions (for
example, ‘likes’ and other emotional responses).
And, perhaps most importantly, we’ll be able to read and respond
to comments.
Fourth, Facebook allows us to frame our ideas in the minds of our
audience and that can have profound downstream effects. (More
on framing later.)
It’s important to mention that TDB is not a ‘how to’ Facebook
advertising course. We’re not going to tell you how to setup
advertising accounts and how to push the right buttons in the
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 13 --

right order. That’s well beyond the scope of this course.
However, we will share several free resources in this module that
will help and we encourage you to review all of them.
Start by reading our 10-part mini-course How To Build A Happy
Customer Creating Engine With Paid Traf�c. That’s a 30,000′ view
of paid traf�c that explores three questions:
▪ What is paid traf�c?
▪ What is a ‘traf�c engine’?
▪ Why should you care?
Next, review the Get started with Facebook and Get started with
advertising content on Facebook’s Blueprint training. Pay
particular attention to Ad Policies for Content, Creative, and
Targeting.
Under Learn advanced buying options, watch Create Facebook
Ads. Then watch all of the content in the section titled Target the
right audience.
When you’re ready to setup your advertising account, review
Facebook’s Ads Manager.
We realize this seems like a lot of additional work. However, it’s
worth it and the time you invest now is time well spent.
(If you are a customer of The Traf�c Engine, re-read module 6.5 —
Facebook Offer and Messaging Testing Framework. That has
direct application to TDB.)
In future TDB modules, we’ll share screencasts of our work for
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 13 --

‘Project X’ (including Facebook setup, hypothesis testing, and
review).
Before we dive in to speci�cs, let’s identify some quicksand we
want you to avoid.
First, avoid language in your ads that calls out people directly.
Conventional wisdom for advertising copy is to identify the
prospects’ pain and then amplify it. Don’t do that on Facebook.
For example, “If you owe the IRS more than $10,000 in back taxes”
might get attention in a banner ad, but it’s not the type of
messaging Facebook wants on its platform.
Second, avoid claims with time frames (even if they’re true). For
example, “lose 10 lbs in 10 days” is a claim (lose 10 lbs) and a time
frame (in 10 days).
There’s a lot of nuance, of course — you might be telling your own
story, for example, and you might have lost 10 lbs in 10 days.
However, claims with time frames seem to have a near-magical
ability to attract negative attention from Facebook and we
recommend avoiding those completely.
Third, write conversationally. Facebook is a social environment
and anything that feels like selling is very noticeable. Don’t try to
‘write copy’ (or, even worse, ‘write AD copy’).
Instead, imagine you’re speaking to an ideal prospect and write
the way you would speak.
(Pro tip: read your ad copy out loud and imagine you’re speaking
to an ideal prospect. That’s a quick way to dramatically improve
your writing.)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 13 --

In addition to avoiding problems, we also want to stack the deck
in our favor. To do that, we’re going to:
▪ Frame our ideas to increase their power.
▪ Create compelling hooks to capture our audience’s attention
immediately.
▪ Use the ‘Rule of 3’ to ensure our ads help prospects
understand our ideas.
Let’s start with framing.
A frame de�nes what’s possible in your world (and what isn’t),
what matters (and what doesn’t). The frame pulls your ideal
prospects forward and repels others (ideally before they even
enter your world).
For example, a typical direct response-style headline might
include the phrase “a little known secret…” That’s a frame that gives
context for the information that follows.
In Rich Schefren’s words, “secrets are a form of social currency”.
We pay attention when we’re told something is a “little known
secret” — that’s why that headline format is so popular. (And
wildly overused, in our opinion.)
The �rst sentence of The Traf�c Engine manifesto is “What you’re
about to read was eighteen months (and twenty-one years) in the making.”
That’s also a frame.
The subtext of that sentence communicates a lot, including:
▪ A lot of thought went into the creation of the course.
▪ It’s based on decades of expertise.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 13 --

▪ The person who created the course is knowledgeable.
None of that was stated explicitly — it’s implied by the frame.
Once you become aware of frames and the power of framing,
you’ll notice frames everywhere.
For our purposes, we’re setting a frame in our Facebook ads that is
in service to the idea we’re validating. For example, we could
frame ‘Project X’ as a way to generate ideas on demand, or we
could create a more narrow, tactical frame for a speci�c audience
(“generate an endless stream of content ideas for your blog…”).
How we frame our ideas affects who is interested in those ideas
and how those ideas are perceived.
Think about a frame as the door someone steps through to get
into your world. If you’re an expert in a particular �eld, then you
can frame what you’re sharing as hard-earned experience /
expertise.
Or, if you created something to scratch your own itch, you can
frame your idea as what you discovered along the way, how you
can save them time because you’ve done the work already,
something counter-intuitive your discovered, etc.
For additional texture, here’s a (lightly edited) excerpt about
frames from our Messaging & Writing Framework.
Frames are written to capture attention, generate interest for the
right audience, and discourage the wrong audience. What’s in it for
them is the underlying structure.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 13 --

A frame captures the promises we’re making to the reader.
A really good frame says, “here’s something interesting … here’s why
it’s interesting … and if you want to learn more, here’s what to do
next”.
Here are a few questions we use when creating a Frame:
▪ What is my audience’s core desire? If they could wave a magic
wand and get exactly what they want, what would it be? (It’s
worth highlighting that part of our job is to “read between the
lines” because people often can’t externalize their deeply felt,
visceral emotional needs.)
▪ What can my audience accomplish better / faster / with less
e�ort and/or lower cost as a result of what I’m going to share?
▪ What is something I know about this topic that my audience
doesn’t that would be interesting and valuable to them?
▪ What is one thing that, if my audience internalized it, would
completely change their perspective? (Think: MIND BLOWN!)
▪ In what ways is conventional wisdom about this topic wrong
or less e�ective?
▪ If someone is interested in the value I have to o�er, what should
s/he do next?
Use these questions as prompts.
The most signi�cant shift that informs how we write (and how we
think about the work we do in general) is that we’re continually re-
orienting to the perspective of the reader.
What does she want most?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 13 --

How can we create the most value for him?
Next up — Facebook Ad ‘Hooks’.
Here’s an excerpt from The Traf�c Engine.
The hook is the �rst two lines of a Facebook ad. That’s my term — I’ve
heard that called by many names.
My two favorite hooks are dialogue and questions (which can be
combined too).
‘You’re never going to believe what my accountant told me about
the Payroll Protection Loan…’
‘I’m sorry Shawn, I talked to your attorney and there’s nothing we
can do to help…’
‘What if everything I believed about gaining muscle after turning
40 was wrong?’
‘Three things I wish every digital marketer knew about paid
advertising on Google and Facebook…’
The opportunities are endless.
The �rst two lines of an ad pre-qualify, so be careful about who and
how you attract. At the same time, remember that the �rst two lines
‘sell’ the rest of the ad. A little conversational curiosity to get a
prospect’s attention is OK, in my opinion, if the ad copy is
transparent.
The Rule of 3 was �rst mentioned during a Momentum Building
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 13 --

Workshop (Lean Edition) landing page review. Shawn realized
during that conversation he hadn’t ever mentioned it to anyone —
including André!
The Rule of 3 can be used for everything you’ll write in this
course. It works at the micro-level (for example, what to include in
a single Facebook ad), and at the macro-level (for example, an idea
you’re validating that includes a Facebook ad, landing page, and
email series).
Here’s an overview (lightly edited to be more speci�cally relevant
for TDB).
Enter Shawn…
(BTW, I’m sure I did not invent this idea — I just gave the framework
a name when I noticed how often I use it.)
Structure is important to me when I write. I want to know where I’m
going and the order in which I’ll get there.
I also want to know that my ideas �ow from one to the next, that I’ve
supported those ideas well, and that there’s a natural progression
from A to B to C.
The Rule of 3 starts with a controlling idea / thesis statement (this is
the main point I’m making — for TDB, this is the idea you’re
validating). Then, I make three main points that are each
accompanied by three supporting ideas.
Finally, I want to direct the reader (or viewer) to what’s next.
Structurally, I imagine it like this:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 13 --

Controlling Idea / Thesis Statement
�. Main point #1.
�. Supporting idea #1.
�. Supporting idea #2.
�. Supporting idea #3.
�. Main point #2.
�. Supporting idea #1.
�. Supporting idea #2.
�. Supporting idea #3.
�. Main point #3.
�. Supporting idea #1.
�. Supporting idea #2.
�. Supporting idea #3.
Call to Action
This framework works for writing and editing.
For example, if you’re writing a 700-900-word Facebook ad, start with
the main takeaway you want the reader to believe (that’s your
controlling idea / whatever you’re trying to validate).
Then, identify the three things that person needs to believe in order to
accept your controlling idea.
Make sure those ideas make sense and that they follow logically from
one to the other. (They’re not just random ideas thrown on the page.)
Next, support each of those three main points with (up to) three proof
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 13 --

points.
Finally, after you’ve made your case (like a prosecutor), tell the reader
what to do next.
If you’re editing your work or another person’s work, map their ideas
to the ‘rule of 3’ structure. (This can be an eye-opening exercise.)
▪ Is the controlling idea obvious?
▪ Do the main points �ow?
▪ Are they supported with evidence?
▪ Does the CTA lead naturally to what’s next?
I suspect some of you are wondering “does it have to be three main
points and three supporting ideas?”
No. These are guidelines. However, 3 x 3 works very well in most
circumstances. Judgment and discretion are required.
And yes, for those of you who are astute observers, the Rule of 3 is
fractal.
There are three parts (controlling idea, making the case, call to
action), and within each part are three additional parts.
(If you’re writing much longer copy, you might include three sub-
points under each supporting idea, for example.)
It’s threes within threes within threes. (Our brains seem to understand
groups of three well.)
The key to this framework’s e�ectiveness is its simplicity. Don’t
overthink it!
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 13 --

OK, let’s translate all of this into action with the following
homework assignment.
�. Read this module once, beginning to end, to get a general
sense of what we’re discussing.
�. Re-read this module a second time, and take notes. Identify
any questions you have (ask those in the comments below).
�. Determine where you want to start — Facebook account
setup or writing your �rst ad.
�. If you want to setup your Facebook account �rst, review the
free paid traf�c mini-course and Facebook Blueprint
training above. Take notes and, when you’re ready, setup
your Facebook advertising account.
�. When you’re ready to create your �rst Facebook ad, identify
your controlling idea �rst. What is the main takeaway
someone needs to believe in order to take a step closer
toward your idea (by opting in, scheduling a call, etc.)? Spend
however much time it takes to get this right because
everything that follows is affected by your controlling idea.
�. Clearly identify the frame you’re using to present your
perspective to your audience.
�. Follow the ‘Rule of 3’ to write a 700-900 word conversational
Facebook ad. Imagine you’re speaking to an ideal prospect.
�. Make sure your ad has a compelling hook that attracts
attention.
www.getwsodo.com
